data.title: Beyond US-China decoupling, self-reliance is now a global movement
data.description:
My recent visit to Mianyang, the second-largest city in Sichuan province, revealed a place little known despite being just a 90-minute drive from Chengdu. Its unique distinction lies in its high concentration of scientists – a legacy of its role as a central hub during the “Third Front Construction” from 1964 to 1978. This was a critical period when China relocated strategic industries inland to build a self-reliant economy against a backdrop of external threats.
This era formed the second of three key phases in China’s industrial evolution, which began with establishing a heavy industrial foundation in the east during the 1950s, moved to the defensive Third Front Construction in the country’s interior, and culminated in the post-1980s era of reform and opening up.
During the Third Front era, millions of people and advanced research institutes were relocated from coastal areas – mainly Shanghai and Beijing – to the inland western region, with many facilities and factories built directly into mountainsides. Mianyang was dramatically transformed.
This legacy is the foundation for its unique status as China’s only official Science and Technology City. It is a state-mandated hub specialising in sectors ranging from national defence, aerospace and nuclear technology to electronics, advanced materials and smart robotics.
My trip served as a powerful reminder of the shifting global economic order. It is a profound irony that merely 60 years after China’s initial self-reliance campaign, the world is turning away from super-globalisation and stepping into a new age of self-reliance.
This shift is primarily driven by a universal desire for greater economic stability and national security. No longer just a feature of China’s development strategy, the trend has become widespread, evident even within Western nations.


Facing persistent trade headwinds and geopolitical complexities, China has elevated economic self-reliance to a core national priority. The country began working to reduce its dependence on the US over a decade ago, aiming to pre-empt a blockade of critical Western technologies. From Beijing’s perspective, this strategic apprehension has been validated by the West’s accelerated “de-risking” and supply chain decoupling.
But China’s current self-reliance strategy is fundamentally different from its historical precedent. Unlike the isolated and often inefficient Third Front campaign, the policy now maintains a open-door commitment. The primary objective is not to achieve total economic separation but to cultivate a robust domestic innovation ecosystem to lessen external dependencies and overcome strategic chokepoint vulnerabilities.
Despite the US tightening restrictions on academic collaboration and limiting Chinese access to sensitive fields like artificial intelligence, demand for an American education among Chinese students remains undiminished. US President Donald Trump has warned that blocking Chinese and other international students could cause half the colleges in the US to “go out of business”.
Chinese capital continues to seek investment opportunities in the US. Yet, this intent often meets rising obstacles. For two consecutive summers, I have encountered hi-tech executives from China in Manhattan actively seeking to invest in the robust US tech sector, only to find increasing barriers.
The US has significantly escalated export controls on China since 2018, imposing restrictions on advanced semiconductors and computing. Beijing’s calls for continued technological collaboration are being met with growing caution and fear in the West.


The European Union is moving to force member states to strip Huawei Technologies and ZTE from their telecoms networks. Even Chinese-made electric buses are facing scrutiny, as Norway, Denmark and Britain launch security probes into vehicles from Yutong, the world’s largest bus manufacturer. The investigations centre on the speculative claim that these buses could be remotely deactivated, fuelling national security fears.
Thus, a central dilemma for China’s new self-reliance is to find a viable path for global engagement, even as the international environment grows more restrictive and wary.
The US strategic pivot to reduce dependence on Chinese manufacturing did not begin with recent tariffs but was set in motion during Trump’s first term. Since at least 2018, the US has developed policies to lessen its reliance on China. Initially, many Western companies were reluctant to dismantle their well-established supply chains in China. But the persistent trade instability has forced businesses to reconsider the risks.
Despite the truce agreed at the recent Xi-Trump summit, the shadow of trade war continues to loom. General Motors has reportedly instructed thousands of its suppliers to find alternatives to Chinese-sourced raw materials and parts, setting a 2027 deadline for some to dissolve their China-based sourcing. The effects of trade war are visible on the ground in China’s major manufacturing hubs, like the Greater Bay Area and Yangtze River Delta, where many factories, including newly built ones, stand empty.


High tariffs in the US and Europe are prompting China to offshore its manufacturing. This strategy, essentially a Chinese version of “China Plus N”, ensures the country’s continued trade growth by relocating production abroad while retaining core technology domestically.
The US has its version of “China Plus N”, aiming to reduce its massive supply chain dependence on China and regain control over critical industries. The overarching goal is to diversify production away from China, bolstering economic security and resilience.
Ironically, the parallel pursuit of “China Plus N” strategies by both the West and China, though born of opposing perspectives, may reduce mutual dependence to a level that assures both sides’ strategic peace of mind. As the major economies decouple, smaller nations are poised to integrate into the evolving global supply chain, benefiting from relocated manufacturing and investment.

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest man over stealing credit cards on flight from Abu Dhabi
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a man for allegedly stealing five credit cards from the luggage of two passengers as they slept on a flight from Abu Dhabi to the city, the Post has learned.
A source said on Friday that the man, 52, was arrested a day earlier at Hong Kong International Airport after two passengers filed reports with authorities.
The insider said another passenger, 41, had witnessed the suspect take the credit cards from a 34-year-old woman’s luggage stored in an overhead compartment as she slept on the flight from the United Arab Emirates.
The woman woke up to find the suspect holding her bag and two of her credit cards placed on a seat, the source said.
According to the source, the witness alerted crew members after he saw the suspect take something out of his trouser pocket and place it on the seat, with the item turning out to be three credit cards wrapped in a piece of tissue paper.
The three cards belonged to a 37-year-old man, the insider said.
The suspect, reportedly a truck driver from mainland China, was arrested on suspicion of theft and was detained as part of police’s investigation.
The source said the man had denied committing any wrongdoing.
In late July, police arrested three men for in-flight theft after three Indian nationals reported losing cash and property during a flight from India to Hong Kong.
Under the Theft Ordinance, a person could be sentenced to a maximum of 10 years in jail for theft.

data.title: Will US approval for arms sales to India pave the way for a trade deal?
data.description:
Washington’s approval of a US$93 million arms deal for India signals warming ties between the two sides after months of trade tensions and a potential prelude to a visit by US President Donald Trump to New Delhi next year, according to analysts.
The supply of Javelin anti-tank missiles and Excalibur precision-guided artillery rounds to India comes as the two countries seek to end a dispute over US tariffs and negotiate a broad trade agreement.
Analysts say the weapons purchase could help reduce India’s trade surplus with the US and give Trump a tangible deliverable ahead of a trip to Delhi.
Vivek Mishra, deputy director of the strategic studies programme at the Observer Research Foundation, said “the timing is interesting because we have been engaged in trade negotiations with the US and the deal will be an element to show how much money Trump can pull from India”.
“Overall, it is good for US-India ties,” he told This Week in Asia, noting the deal followed Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s visit to the US in February and the renewal of a 10-year bilateral defence framework last month.
Expectations of Trump’s visit have raised hopes in Delhi that the two sides could conclude a trade agreement to lower import tariffs on Indian goods from around 50 per cent to below 20 per cent.
Earlier this week, state-owned Indian oil companies signed a one-year deal to import around 2.2 million tons of liquefied petroleum gas from the US Gulf coast, amounting to around 10 per cent of India’s LPG imports, which are expected to help trim its trade surplus with the US.
India has typically enjoyed a surplus in its goods trade with the US. Last year, the US recorded a goods trade deficit of US$45.8 billion with India. In comparison, the world’s largest economy had a services surplus of US$102 million in the two-way trade in the same year.

The arms and LPG deals could help conclude trade talks between Delhi and Washington without India having to open politically sensitive sectors, such as agriculture, wider to American goods, according to analysts.
Priyajit Debsarkar, a London-based political and security analyst, said the arms deal was an indication that both sides were laying the ground for Trump’s visit to India next year.
“It is a signal to the Maga [Make America Great Again] vote base of Mr Trump that India is trying to improve its trade relations with the US and hence it is buying American weapons,” he said.
Another message that Washington wants to convey from the deal is that it is committed to defence cooperation with Delhi, including within the framework of the Quadrilateral Security Dialogue, under which the US, India, Japan and Australia are members, according to Debsarkar.
The type of weapons sought by India highlighted that it wanted to boost its deterrence capabilities against Pakistan following their brief conflict in May, Debsarkar said.
India-Russia ties
Even as India seeks to boost its US weaponry, it would continue to buy arms from Russia, its biggest supplier, analysts say.
Russian President Vladimir Putin is reportedly expected to visit Delhi next month, with new arms sales to India likely on the agenda.

India has reduced its purchases of discounted Russian crude following Washington’s sanctions against key oil producers in Russia. About half of the 50 per cent tariffs imposed by the US on India were linked to Delhi’s purchases of Russian oil.
“If you are winding down energy imports from Russia, you will need to look around and expand in other areas, so defence could be one of them,” Mishra said.
India is said to be considering buying more of Russia’s S-400 Triumf missiles, which played a critical role in repelling Pakistani attacks during the conflict in May. It is also looking to buy SU-57 fifth-generation stealth fighter jets, according to local media reports.
In recent years, India has been diversifying its arms suppliers, buying more weapons from the US, France, the UK and Israel in addition to Russia.
Since the outbreak of the war in Ukraine, Western countries have been pressuring India to distance itself from Russia. Trump has been pushing for an end to the conflict with his 28-point peace proposal.
In the event of an end to the war, Washington is likely to put less pressure on Delhi’s ties with Moscow, and this could in turn pave the way for an improvement in the India-US relationship, Mishra said.

data.title: More than half of Hong Kong pupils get desired Primary One spot, a 20-year high
data.description:
More than half of the children set to start Primary One at Hong Kong’s public or government-subvented schools in the next academic year have secured a place at their preferred institutions, the highest success rate in about 20 years.
The Education Bureau revealed on Friday that 37,581 children had applied for discretionary places in government and aided primary schools this year for admission next September.
Among them, 19,656 or 52.3 per cent will receive a place in these schools – a record high since 2005.
In 2024, the number of pupils securing a spot in their preferred schools stood at 49.4 per cent, or 20,644 applicants.
In 2005, among a total of 51,178 applicants, 26,032, or about 51 per cent, received a place at their preferred schools.
Competition has decreased in recent years amid a shrinking student population and the city’s declining birth rates.
The number of children applying for places at public or aided schools this year also saw a record low, with the number falling below 40,000 for the first time.
Compared with last year’s figures, the number of applicants this time dropped by 4,200, or roughly one-tenth, from 41,781.
The number used to hit almost 70,000 in the late 1990s.
In Hong Kong, Primary One places at public and aided schools are allocated using a two-stage system.
The results of the first stage, known as the discretionary admission phase, for the 2026-27 academic year will be released next Monday.
The second part, the central allocation system, will be conducted next year.
Under the system, each government or aided primary school may make use of about 50 per cent of its total Primary One places as discretionary places. The remaining 50 per cent of the total places will be reserved for central allocation.

According to the government, the Primary One admission scheme is designed to ease the pressure on parents and their children to enter popular primary schools.
Under the system, the city is divided into some 30 school nets.
Parents have to indicate their applicant children’s residential address in the application form.
Those who have not been offered a discretionary place will participate in the central allocation at a later stage.
Direct Subsidy Scheme primary schools, private primary schools and primary schools of the English Schools Foundation are excluded from the system.
Parents who wish their children to enrol in these schools may apply directly to them.
Upon receiving the discretionary place results next Monday, parents should register their children next Wednesday or Thursday at the school they applied to during school hours, a bureau spokesman said.
“Parents who cannot register their child on the above dates should notify the person-in-charge of the school in advance to make alternative arrangements. Otherwise, they will be deemed to have given up their discretionary place,” the spokesman added.

data.title: Indian Air Force pilot dies in Tejas jet crash during Dubai Air Show demonstration
data.description:
The pilot of an Indian combat plane died after the aircraft crashed on Friday during a demonstration flight for spectators at the Dubai Air Show, the Indian Air Force said.
The Indian HAL Tejas, a combat aircraft used in the Indian Air Force, crashed at around 2.10pm after the pilot had flown across the site of the biennial air show in Dubai several times.
The plane appeared to lose control and dive directly towards the ground just prior to crashing inside the grounds of the airfield.
The Indian Air Force confirmed the crash in a statement and said “the pilot sustained fatal injuries in the accident”.
“IAF deeply regrets the loss of life and stands firmly with the bereaved family in this time of grief,” it said, adding that “a court of inquiry is being constituted, to ascertain the cause of the accident”.

Black smoke rose over the Al Maktoum International Airport at Dubai World Central as a crowd of spectators watched and sirens sounded after the crash.
Dubai police and airport officials offered no immediate comment.
The city state’s second airport was hosting the biennial Dubai Air Show, which has seen major aircraft orders by both the long-haul carrier Emirates and its lower-cost sister airline FlyDubai.
Tejas is India’s indigenous fighter aircraft, built by state-run Hindustan Aeronautics (HAL). The lightweight, single-engine jet is expected to bolster India’s depleted fighter fleet as China expands its military presence in South Asia, including by strengthening defence ties with India’s rival, Pakistan.
In September, India’s Defence Ministry signed a contract with HAL to procure 97 Tejas jets for the air force. Deliveries are expected to begin in 2027.
The Indian government also signed a deal with HAL in 2021 for 83 Tejas aircraft. Deliveries, expected last year, have been delayed largely because of shortages of engines that must be imported from the United States.

data.title: China’s aerospace giants seek to boost arms sales abroad at global air shows
data.description:
China’s aerospace manufacturers have become a growing presence at overseas air shows as the country seeks to expand its arms sales abroad, bolstered by technological advances in the defence and aviation industries.
More than 1,500 companies took part in this year’s Dubai Airshow, the largest air show in the Middle East, including around 80 Chinese companies, such as China National Aero-Technology Import & Export Corporation (Catic).
Catic is the international arms sales subsidiary and the global representative of the Aviation Industry Corporation of China (AVIC), a major Chinese military aircraft manufacturer.
At the Dubai Airshow, Catic displayed miniature and life-size models of Chinese military aircraft at its indoor and outdoor booths.
Its two most advanced fighter jets – the 4.5-generation J-10CE and fifth-generation J-35A – were prominently displayed. The Y-20 large transport aircraft, the L-15A training aircraft, Wing Loong series drones and helicopters were also present for the Middle Eastern audience, which included many in military uniform.
At its outdoor venue, Catic displayed a model of the Wing Loong-X unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV), an export variant of the fixed-wing combat Wing Loong drone series. The drones have been widely exported to Middle Eastern countries including Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates and Egypt, along with missiles that can be launched from the aircraft.
The aerospace giant has had a growing presence at overseas defence exhibitions as it aims to boost overseas arms sales amid growing international interest in Chinese weapons systems.

Chinese company officials present at these trade shows have typically refrained from speaking to the media, rejecting inquiries from reporters.
However, a slight shift in attitude was observed at the Dubai Airshow, with the Chinese exhibitors engaging in limited but relatively open contact with the media.
It was not the first time Catic has displayed the J-10CE and J-35 in the UAE. The two advanced fighters were also shown at the company’s booth during the International Defence Exhibition and Conference in Abu Dhabi in February.
Catic was also present at the Paris Air Show, one of the largest air shows in the world, in June to promote its latest products to the European market, despite China’s lack of arms exports to Nato member states.
In the armed confrontation between India and Pakistan near the disputed Kashmir region in May, the Pakistan Air Force’s Chinese-made J-10C shot down India’s Rafale fighter jet. It was the first case of a Chinese fighter conducting an air-to-air kill against Western systems, increasing the interest in Chinese military aircraft among international visitors to the Paris and Dubai air shows.
However, unlike the 2023 edition of the Dubai Airshow or the World Defence Show in Riyadh in February last year, which featured the People’s Liberation Army Air Force’s Ba Yi Aerobatics Team, no Chinese aerobatics teams were present at air shows outside China this year.
Instead, the United Arab Emirates Air Force’s aerobatics team, the Fursan Al Emarat, performed a flying demonstration using its newly acquired L-15 training jets on the first day of this year’s Dubai Airshow.
The UAE signed a deal with Catic to purchase 12 units of the L-15 during the Dubai Airshow in 2023, with an option to purchase additional aircraft.
The 29-minute flying display was the first public demonstration of the UAE aerobatics team using the L-15s, replacing the Italian-made Leonardo MB-339 trainers it had used since the team’s formation in 2008.
The L-15, known as the JL-10 in the PLA, is an advanced jet trainer developed by Hongdu Aviation Industry Corporation with design input from Russia’s Yakovlev, the designer of the Yak-130 trainer. The aircraft delivered to the UAE were configured for the aerobatic role.
During the flying demonstration, seven of the Fursan Al Emarat’s L-15s, painted in black and gold, flew in formation. They showcased a series of gravity-defying manoeuvres, leaving red, green, white, and black smoke trails – the colours of the UAE flag – over the sky.
The Dubai Airshow also featured flying demonstrations by commercial aircraft, including China’s C919 narrowbody airliner, which was first introduced to Chinese airlines in 2023.

data.title: Widodo hails Southeast Asia’s rise as ‘global force’ in entrepreneurship
data.description:
Across Southeast Asia, millions of young people are launching start-ups and driving rapid digitalisation, with new unicorns emerging in a sign that the region is becoming a global force in entrepreneurship, according to former Indonesian president Joko Widodo.
Speaking at the closing of the Bloomberg New Economy Forum on Friday, Widodo said the next unicorn – meaning a privately held start-up valued at more than US$1 billion – might emerge not from Silicon Valley or Shenzhen but from Jakarta, Singapore, Bangkok, Kuala Lumpur, Manila or Hanoi.
“Southeast Asia is no longer just a market, it is becoming a global force,” he said.
He urged countries to ready themselves for what he called an “intelligent economy” by prioritising the free flow of data and accelerating the adoption of technologies such as artificial intelligence, arguing that these would reshape production, strategy and ultimately income levels.
According to equity management software firm Eqvista, in May this year there were more than 1,550 unicorn companies globally and 44 were based in Southeast Asia.
Singapore leads with 22 unicorn start-ups, followed by Indonesia with 10 and Vietnam with five.


Widodo said Indonesia’s experience showed how regulatory clarity and supportive innovation policies could allow local companies to thrive.
Looking ahead, he rejected the idea that AI would wipe out jobs, provided governments ensured workers had the skills to use the technology.
“I really disagree that job opportunities will disappear. In fact, I believe that there will be more jobs and more opportunities to come, if we make sure our people are ready for them,” said Widodo, who served as Indonesian president for a decade from 2014.
He stressed the need for workers to study AI, coding, algorithms and machine learning, describing technological literacy as “fundamental” as national competitiveness increasingly depends on how effectively societies can use data.
International institutions like the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank would have to redefine their financial instruments and systems, and the World Trade Organization would also need to update its rules on trade and tariffs, he said without elaborating.

During Widodo’s tenure, Indonesia’s GDP growth consistently hovered at about 5 per cent, while the country also saw a boost in infrastructure in the form of new airports, ports, dams and toll roads.
“Indonesia’s journey shows that transformation needs courage, persistence and belief. With [a] strong foundation, innovation and global collaboration, Indonesia and Southeast Asia will continue to grow in this new era,” Widodo said.
“In this new economy, the intelligent economy, nations, companies and societies that can integrate intelligence into governance, industries and social systems will grow better and faster.”
Widodo’s call for an “intelligent economy” comes at a moment when Southeast Asia’s start-up sector is facing its harshest funding climate in a decade.
Venture capital funding in Asia totalled just US$51.2 billion in the first nine months of this year, according to accounting firm KPMG. Data from business analytics firm Crunchbase reveals a broader trend: funding hit a decade low of US$65.8 billion last year, a far cry from the record US$194 billion raised in 2021.

data.title: 18 dead in Pakistan glue factory explosion amid safety concerns: ‘like an earthquake’
data.description:
A boiler at a glue-making factory in eastern Pakistan exploded on Friday, killing at least 18 people and injuring 21 others. The force of the blast destroyed the factory building and damaged several nearby homes, igniting a huge fire and spreading panic through the area, officials said.
Police said they arrested the factory manager and were looking for the factory owner, who fled shortly after the explosion at the industrial facility in Faisalabad, a city in Punjab province.
The cause of the explosion was not immediately known, according to Punjab police chief Usman Anwar. Police later said a gas leakage may have caused it.
It was not clear how many of the 18 dead were factory workers and how many were local residents. Earlier reports said at least 15 workers were killed.
Local administrator Raja Jahangir said an investigation was under way to determine how construction permits allowed the factory to be built in a residential area of Faisalabad, in violation of building laws.

The explosion “completely flattened” the factory, Jahangir said, adding that rescuers spent hours pulling bodies and survivors out of the rubble. Several of the injured were in critical condition.
Witnesses said the blast was so powerful that it collapsed nearby homes and hurled debris across the streets.
Punjab Chief Minister Maryam Nawaz Sharif expressed her condolences and ordered authorities to ensure the best possible medical care for the injured.
Muhammad Iqbal, one of the injured at the local hospital, said it felt “like an earthquake” and that “roofs and walls of nearby houses collapsed”. He said his wife and son were also injured, but they were in a stable condition.
Roofs and walls of nearby houses collapsed
Muhammad Iqbal, one of the injured
Nadeem Zafar, 45, said he rushed out of his home after hearing a deafening boom and described the scene of chaos that ensued.
“I saw flames and thick smoke rising,” he said over the phone. “People were screaming, running in every direction, calling for help.”
Ambulances and rescuers arrived quickly and began pulling bodies out of the rubble in the Malikpur neighbourhood, he said, adding that many around him initially thought it was a bomb or some sort of projectile.
Poor safety standards are a frequent cause of industrial accidents and factory fires in Pakistan.
Last year, a dozen workers were injured in a similar boiler explosion at a textile mill in Faisalabad. Also last week, a blast at a firecrackers factory in Pakistan’s port city of Karachi killed four people.

data.title: San Francisco Opera’s The Monkey King is a thrilling landmark in cultural exchange
data.description:
San Francisco Opera’s premiere of The Monkey King is a visually enchanting retelling of the timeless Chinese folk tale and a thrilling landmark in cultural exchange.
With music by Huang Ruo and libretto by David Henry Hwang, this new opera follows the mischievous antics of Sun Wukong, the macaque born from a rock, who discovers this simple truth on his path towards enlightenment and immortality: “You cannot find the land of bliss with a leap and a bound.”
The sets and puppetry by Basil Twist are a wonder and delight from start to finish.
A staggering 4.1 kilometres (2.6 miles) of silk is used to create rippling visions of the undersea kingdom, of flights through the heavens, and of fantastical encounters with magic white horses, hovering jellyfish and moving mountains.
Layers of transparent curtains sweep across the stage, revealing ever more magical worlds of light and colour.
The Monkey character has three forms: a singer in Kang Wang, a puppet who can fly through the air, and a nimble dancer, Huiwang Zhang. Via clever stagecraft, they seamlessly take turns depicting the many aspects of Sun Wukong.

The well-cast singers have glorious voices and sing impeccably. Soprano Meigui Zhang as the goddess Guanyin, suspended in a glowing bubble high above the stage for most of the performance, has a heavenly, crystalline sound. Her calm, wise and benevolent demeanour is perfect for the role.
Tenor Konu Kim as the Jade Emperor struts and sings convincingly. Peixin Chen, as the Supreme Sage Laojun, impresses with his dark, low notes.
Tenor Kang Wang is the ideal Monkey King. He embodies the feisty spirit of Sun Wukong, twirling a magic staff and doing lively leaps and spins. His voice has golden, ringing high notes and a compelling, expressive midrange that is a delight to the ears.
The music does not have set “numbers” but is composed with long lines in the manner established by the German composer Wagner. It is not an opera with musical “hooks” or catchy repeated melodic ideas. It is nevertheless lyrical, but often with a solemn, abstract tone, reminiscent of the chanting of a Greek chorus.
The harmonic language is modern with a persistent level of subtle dissonance, except for certain moments when the clouds part, so to speak, and the discord gives way to concord to great effect.
The pacing is quite varied. Director Diane Paulus tells the story clearly and vividly – not an easy trick for a story combining a superhero monkey with a deeper Buddhist message.

There are lively percussive sections in the dramatic fight and action scenes, including woodblocks and Chinese opera gongs with their strident rising pitch.
The chorus of monkeys has sprightly, chattering music. The orchestra plays lovely, intricate, minimalist-inspired textures to depict flying through the heavens or the undersea realm. In a climactic scene, the orchestra rises to terrifying power as Buddha’s stone hand closes around the Monkey King.
At times, the impact of the singing is limited because the orchestra does not fully support the voices. The singers work hard to express the poignant words with their ringing high notes, but they are acoustically at odds with the orchestra. The moments when they are in clear harmony together are electrifying.
Monkey’s hard-won spiritual growth is movingly depicted, especially near the end when he arrives at what he thinks is the Land of Bliss, or true enlightenment.
Here, the music has a more traditional Chinese sound with the open harmonies of the pentatonic scale, as in the black keys on the piano. It sets the perfect tone for Monkey’s glimpse of what it means to be humble and let go of the ego.
Also near the end, Guanyin has a beautiful passage with solo strings, and her tones ring out with purity as she sings about “relieving the suffering of all creatures regardless of station”.

This tale is so appealing – no wonder a Monkey King video game is so popular – that it is the perfect introduction to Chinese culture for general audiences.
For children, the appeal might be martial arts and comical mischief. For adults, there is a timeless message of compassion. This opera is a welcome landmark moment of connection across cultures: a window into deep Chinese culture, presented by authentic Chinese voices and made accessible to all.
“The Monkey King” by Huang Ruo and David Henry Hwang, San Francisco Opera, until November 30. Reviewed: November 16. On-demand “live stream” viewing online is available from November 23-25.

data.title: Stunning water stage provides backdrop to National Games closing ceremony
data.description:
Honestly, at some point this week while trying to decide how to start this blog, the lyrics from My Way entered my head.
I don’t know if you’ve ever read them properly, but I never used to consider the song particularly morbid, so another learning opportunity there.
Anyway, the sentiment kind of stands. At the end of two action-packed weeks, we’re finally at the end of the National Games and what a fortnight it’s been.
The closing ceremony promises to be one for the ages too, so strap in and let’s see what the final few hours hold.
